title: Quick check: Env vars and secrets

questions.prompt: What happens to NEXT_PUBLIC_ env vars?

choices.text: They're inlined into the JavaScript bundle at build time and shipped to the browser — visible to anyone who views your site's source. Use for non-secret values: Stripe publishable key, Mapbox public token, Google Analytics measurement ID, public config. NEVER use NEXT_PUBLIC_ for secrets — putting `NEXT_PUBLIC_DB_PASSWORD` publishes your database password to every visitor. The 'PUBLIC' in the name is the warning. Validate at startup with Zod or @t3-oss/env-nextjs to catch accidental NEXT_PUBLIC_ on a secret-shaped variable name.

questions.explanation: NEXT_PUBLIC_ vars are inlined into the client bundle. NEVER for secrets.

questions.prompt: Server Action returns `process.env.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` to the client. What's wrong?

choices.text: Even though Server Actions run on the server, their return value is serialized and sent to the client. Returning a secret key transmits it to every caller — same as NEXT_PUBLIC_. The action SHOULD perform the secret-requiring operation server-side (call Stripe with the key, return the result the user needs) and never expose the key itself. Pattern: action takes inputs, uses the secret internally, returns only what the client needs (a payment intent ID, a status, a redirect URL). Never the secret. The 'server-side' guarantee is about WHERE the code runs, not about hiding return values.

questions.explanation: Return values cross to the client. Use secrets server-side; return only what the client needs.
